meta.title: Ancient Mesopotamia
meta.author: Susan Lennox
meta.focusQuestion: What were the important features of ancient Mesopotamia?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: Human beings have been around for a long time. For much of that time, they lived in groups of families or tribes. It took a while for them to form civilized societies. <s>A civilized society</s> <v>is</v> <o>one with rules that help people live and work together</o>. One of the earliest civilized societies was found in ancient Mesopotamia. <s>Mesopotamia</s> <v>was located</v> <o>where Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait are now</o>. <s>This "land between two rivers"</s> <v>was</v> <o>the place where civilization was born</o>.

sections.title: The Ubaid
paragraphs: Ancient Mesopotamian civilization started between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers. That land came to be known as the Fertile Crescent. The first people to live in Mesopotamia settled the southern part of that region. Today, this area is Iraq and Kuwait.

In modern times, ancient stone tools, bricks, and pottery were found near the Euphrates River. These items told stories about the lives of the early people. Researchers learned that farming settlements began there as early as 5000 bce. They named these early settlers the Ubaid after the site where the items were discovered.

The Ubaid were the first people to settle the land known as Sumer. <s>Before the Ubaid formed settlements</s>, <s>they</s> <v>were</v> <o>nomadic hunters and gatherers</o>. <s>They</s> <v>roamed</v> <o>from place to place in search of food and water</o>.

<s>In time</s>, <s>the Ubaid</s> <v>found</v> <o>ways to live in one place</o>. <s>They</s> <v>drained</v> <o>marshes</o> and <v>grew</v> <o>crops</o> in the rich soil. <s>They</s> <v>built</v> <o>canals to bring water to drier areas</o>. <s>They</s> <v>made</v> <o>pottery from river clay</o>.

<s>The river</s> <v>did not only meet</v> <o>their personal needs</o>. <s>It</s> <v>also gave</v> <o>the Ubaid goods to trade with others</o>. <s>Trade routes from Asia and Europe</s> <v>went</v> <o>through the rich plains of Sumeria</o>. <s>Traders</s> <v>provided</v> <o>items that the Ubaid could not grow or make themselves</o>.

sections.title: Uruk, the First City
paragraphs: Over the years, there were more settlements in the Fertile Crescent. Some grew to become true cities. <s>A number</s> <v>became</v> <o>city-states</o>. <s>The most important of these</s> <v>was</v> <o>Uruk (modern-day Warka, Iraq)</o>. <s>Uruk</s> <v>had</v> <o>up to eighty thousand people</o>. <s>A huge ziggurat, or temple,</s> <v>was</v> <o>a center of worship</o>.

<s>Uruk</s> <v>was</v> <o>where Mesopotamian pictographs first appeared</o>. <s>Pictographs</s> <v>are</v> <o>symbols used in writing</o>. <s>Over time</s>, <s>pictographs</s> <v>changed</v> <o>into a system of writing known as cuneiform</o>.

<s>This phonologic system</s> <v>had</v> <o>several advantages over a pictographic system</o>. <s>The system</s> <v>also made</v> <o>it easier to express abstract concepts that would be difficult to show as pictures</o>.

Clay tablets marked with cuneiform symbols gave information about Sumerian life during that time. The people of Uruk and other city-states lived in organized societies. Women had many of the same rights as men. At the highest level of society was a priest-king. That person led others, such as a leader of the plow, leader of the law, and so on. City rulers used trade to show how important they were. Having rare and valuable items was a way to show off their power.

One of the most famous rulers was King Gilgamesh (Bilgames in Sumerian). <s>He</s> <v>was said to be</v> <o>a demigod, the son of a human king and a Sumerian mother-goddess</o>. <s>Gilgamesh</s> <v>is known for</v> <o>building the huge wall that protected Uruk</o>. <s>That wall</s> <v>still stands</v> <o>today</o>.

In 2003, German scientists found a tomb in a Euphrates riverbed that they thought belonged to Gilgamesh. It was similar to the tomb described in the epic. The Epic of Gilgamesh is a collection of ancient Mesopotamian poems. It tells of the exciting adventures of an ancient king's battles and victories. It is thought to be the oldest epic in history.

sections.title: Warring City-States
paragraphs: As the Sumerian city-states grew and thrived, they began to fight with each other for control of the region. War became more and more common.

The earliest detailed record of Sumerian warfare shows a battle between the city-states of Lagash and Umma in 2525 bce. <s>Figures on an upright stone post called a stele</s> <v>show</v> <o>soldiers carrying swords, spears, and shields into battle</o>.

The Sumerians' constant fighting stopped when King Sargon of Akkad (in southern Mesopotamia) took control of the region in about 2340 bce. <s>He</s> <v>united</v> <o>the city-states into the Akkadian Empire</o>.

The Akkadians did not speak Sumerian. However, they used Sumerian cuneiform script to write in their own language. The earliest Sumerian writings in which the author was known were poems by Sargon's daughter Enheduanna. She served as high priestess in the city of Ur.

sections.title: Prosperity and Advancement
paragraphs: Peace during the Akkadian empire brought progress to Mesopotamia. Under Akkadian rule, Mesopotamia created the first mail system. Clay tablets were marked with cuneiform messages, then wrapped in clay envelopes. An envelope was pressed with the seal of the sender and the name and address of the receiver. The clay envelope had to be broken in order to get to the message inside.

A central government carried out laws and regulations. Trade grew and expanded. Business deals used cylinder seals—carved tubes that were pressed into clay. Pressing one's seal into the clay was like signing one's name today.

Around 3500 bce, the Sumerians began using a round flat mechanical device to make clay bowls. The potter's wheel sat horizontally on an axis and, when spun, allowed the potter to make a bowl with even edges and surfaces. After much experimenting, the Sumerians tried attaching one wheel to each side of a single axle. The result made it easy to carry loads. Other civilizations copied the Sumerians' invention, and wheels were soon used across the world.

The Mesopotamians also developed the idea of numerical place value. The way we measure time uses this same system from long ago. We use a system created by the ancient Mesopotamians—base sixty. There are sixty seconds in one minute and sixty minutes in one hour.

sections.title: Fall of an Empire
paragraphs: The Akkadian Empire ended in about 2150 bce after many revolts and an invasion by the Gutians (GOO-tee-uhnz). <s>The Gutians</s> <v>came</v> <o>from the mountains to the north</o> and <v>held</v> <o>regular raids on the outer parts of the kingdom</o>. <s>In time</s>, <s>the Gutian nomads</s> <v>took over</v> <o>Akkad</o>, and <s>the Akkadian Empire</s> <v>collapsed</v>.

<s>The Gutians</s> <v>let</v> <o>the canals fall apart</o> and <v>brought on</v> <o>a terrible famine</o>. <s>Their 125-year rule of Sumer</s> <v>led to</v> <o>what came to be known as the Dark Age of Mesopotamia</o>.

vocabulary.definition: existing as or relating to an idea rather than a physical thing; not concrete
vocabulary.sentence: <s>The system</s> <v>made</v> <o>it easier to express abstract concepts that would be difficult to show as pictures</o>.

options: something that is physical and solid and can be touched easily
relating to an idea rather than a physical thing you can touch
something that is rare and valuable like gold or precious gems
something that is ancient and old from many thousands of years ago

vocabulary.definition: cities or urban areas that act as independent countries

options: large empires that controlled many nations and people by force
cities that act as independent countries with their own government and laws
small farming villages where people grew crops and raised animals
mountain fortresses built to protect soldiers during times of war and conflict

vocabulary.definition: having an advanced level of social order and cultural development
vocabulary.sentence: <s>A civilized society</s> <v>is</v> <o>one with rules that help people live and work together</o>.

options: having an advanced level of social order and cultural development
living in small tribes that moved from place to place following animals
moving from place to place without ever having a permanent home anywhere
fighting wars against neighboring cities to gain more land and resources

vocabulary.definition: a system of writing made up of wedge-shaped characters, used in parts of the ancient Middle East
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Over time</s>, <s>pictographs</s> <v>changed</v> <o>into a system of writing known as cuneiform</o>.

options: a type of temple used for religious worship in ancient times
a system of writing made up of wedge-shaped characters on clay
a metal weapon used by soldiers in battles against their enemies
a farming tool used to plant seeds in the rich soil near rivers

vocabulary.definition: a being from mythology who is part human and part god
vocabulary.sentence: <s>He</s> <v>was said to be</v> <o>a demigod, the son of a human king and a Sumerian mother-goddess</o>.

options: a powerful king who ruled over a large empire with many soldiers
a being from mythology who is part human and part god in nature
a temple priest who served the gods and led religious ceremonies
a famous warrior who won many battles against enemy city-states

vocabulary.definition: a collection of nations or people ruled by one person or government

options: a single city with its own government and laws for its people
a collection of nations or people ruled by one person or government
a religious ceremony held to honor the gods and ask for their blessing
a type of writing used to record important events and trade deals

vocabulary.definition: moving from place to place without a permanent home
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Before the Ubaid formed settlements</s>, <s>they</s> <v>were</v> <o>nomadic hunters and gatherers</o>.

options: living in large cities with many buildings and a central government
moving from place to place without a permanent home in search of food
farming the land and growing crops in one location for many years
building temples and ziggurats to worship the gods of the city

vocabulary.definition: of or relating to the study of speech sounds within or among languages
vocabulary.sentence: <s>This phonologic system</s> <v>had</v> <o>several advantages over a pictographic system</o>.

options: speech sounds within or among languages and how they are produced
ancient temples built to honor the gods and serve as places of worship
farming techniques used to grow crops in the rich soil near rivers
trade routes that connected distant cities and carried valuable goods

vocabulary.definition: symbols or pictures that represent words or ideas

options: symbols or pictures that represent words or ideas in early writing systems
types of ancient weapons used by soldiers in battles against enemies
kings and rulers who governed the city-states of ancient Mesopotamia
trade goods that were carried along routes from distant cities and lands

vocabulary.sentence: <s>The Gutians</s> <v>came</v> from the mountains to the north and <v>held</v> <o>regular raids on the outer parts of the kingdom</o>.

options: peaceful trading between neighboring cities and their local merchants
sudden invasions or surprise attacks on the outer parts of the kingdom
religious ceremonies held to honor the gods and ask for their protection
farming methods used to grow crops in the rich soil near the rivers

vocabulary.definition: a large, upright slab or column of rock, usually inscribed with designs or words
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Figures on an upright stone post called a stele</s> <v>show</v> <o>soldiers carrying swords, spears, and shields into battle</o>.

options: a type of weapon used by soldiers to fight in battles against enemies
a large, upright stone slab with inscribed designs recording events
a religious temple where people went to worship the gods of the city
a farming tool used to plant seeds in the fields near the rivers

vocabulary.definition: a rectangular structure in ancient Mesopotamia with steps up the sides and a temple at the top
vocabulary.sentence: <s>A huge ziggurat, or temple,</s> <v>was</v> <o>a center of worship</o>.

options: a rectangular structure with steps up the sides and a temple at the top
a type of metal used to make weapons and tools for everyday use
a farming village where people lived and worked the land for crops
a trade route that connected distant cities and carried valuable goods

questions.question: Mesopotamia was located ___.
questions.evidence: Mesopotamia was located where Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait are now.
choices: where the United States is now with its many large cities
where Greece, Italy, and Spain are now in southern Europe
where Egypt and the great pyramids are now in northeastern Africa
where Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait are now in the Middle East
questions.explanation: Mesopotamia was located in the area of modern-day Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait.

questions.question: The book states that it took a long time for humans to form civilized societies. A civilized society is ___.
questions.evidence: A civilized society is one with rules that help people live and work together.
choices: one that moves from place to place without a permanent home anywhere
one with rules and laws that help people live together in an organized way
one with people who are hunters and others who are gatherers of food
one that is peaceful and where everyone always agrees about everything
questions.explanation: A civilized society has rules and laws that help people live and work together.

questions.question: Why did ancient leaders desire to have rare and valuable items?
questions.evidence: City rulers used trade to show how important they were. Having rare and valuable items was a way to show off their power.
choices: to share with their people and improve the quality of life for everyone
to show off their power and demonstrate how important they were as rulers
to use them to pay their taxes to the government and fund public projects
to show the quality of the work produced by the people of their city-state
questions.explanation: Ancient leaders used rare and valuable items to show off their power and importance.

questions.question: What can you conclude about the people of Uruk?
questions.evidence: A huge ziggurat, or temple, was a center of worship.
choices: They had no system of communication and could not write anything down.
They preferred to live in the countryside rather than in the large city.
They did not like to follow the laws and often broke the rules of society.
They were religious people who built a huge temple as a center of worship.
questions.explanation: The people of Uruk built a huge ziggurat (temple) as a center of worship, showing they were religious.

questions.question: Why was the Gutian rule of Sumer known as the Dark Age of Mesopotamia?
questions.evidence: The Gutians let the canals fall apart and brought on a terrible famine. Their 125-year rule of Sumer led to what came to be known as the Dark Age of Mesopotamia.
choices: A large sand storm blocked out the Sun, and all the crops died in the fields.
Many people were taken as slaves and lost their homes and families forever.
The land and canals were not taken care of, and many people starved to death.
All the artwork was destroyed, and people were not allowed to be creative at all.
questions.explanation: The Gutians let the canals fall apart, causing famine. This period became known as the Dark Age.

questions.question: What was King Sargon's influence on the Sumerians?
questions.evidence: The Sumerians' constant fighting stopped when King Sargon of Akkad took control of the region in about 2340 bce. He united the city-states into the Akkadian Empire. Sargon kept order by placing key trusted allies in positions of power.
choices: He encouraged fighting between city-states because he enjoyed being in battle.
He enslaved anyone who participated in war and forced them to work for him.
He stopped the fighting and chose trusted leaders to help him keep the peace.
He stopped all trade between city-states and therefore stopped all fighting too.
questions.explanation: King Sargon stopped the constant fighting between city-states and united them, placing trusted allies in power to keep peace.

questions.question: After reading the section 'Prosperity and Advancement,' what can you conclude about Mesopotamia?
questions.evidence: Peace during the Akkadian empire brought progress to Mesopotamia. Under Akkadian rule, Mesopotamia created the first mail system.
choices: Times of peace allowed people in Mesopotamia to create new systems, such as a mail system.
Money made during wartimes helped create a numerical place value system for trade.
The people of Mesopotamia resettled in new places based on an increase in trade routes.
During times of peace, there was little money for the people, so leaders encouraged war.
questions.explanation: Peace brought progress — Mesopotamia created the first mail system, the potter's wheel, and numerical place value during peaceful times.

questions.question: Read this sentence: 'Before the Ubaid formed settlements, they were nomadic hunters and gatherers.' Which sentence from the text helps readers understand the meaning of the word 'nomadic'?
questions.evidence: They roamed from place to place in search of food and water.
choices: The Ubaid were the first people to settle the land known as Sumer in ancient times.
They named these early settlers the Ubaid after the site where items were discovered.
They roamed from place to place in search of food and water for their families.
They built canals to bring water to drier areas so they could grow crops there.
questions.explanation: 'Roamed from place to place' explains that 'nomadic' means moving around without a permanent home.

questions.question: Some settlements in the Fertile Crescent became city-states. Which of the following is true of a city-state?
choices: It acts like its own independent country with its own government and set of laws.
It does not have any laws and people are free to do whatever they want to do.
It does not allow trade with other cities and keeps all its goods for its own people.
It is run by a president who is elected by the people who live in the city-state.
questions.explanation: A city-state is a city that acts like its own independent country with its own government and laws.

questions.question: The name Mesopotamia means ___.
questions.evidence: This "land between two rivers" was the place where civilization was born.
choices: the land between two rivers that flow through the fertile plains of the region
the land between two mountain ranges where people lived in small cave dwellings
the land between two seas where fishermen caught fish and traded with other people
the land between two deserts where travelers crossed on camels carrying valuable goods
questions.explanation: Mesopotamia means 'land between two rivers' — the Tigris and Euphrates.

questions.question: In what ways did trade influence the culture of ancient Mesopotamia?
questions.evidence: Trade routes from Asia and Europe went through the rich plains of Sumeria. Traders provided items that the Ubaid could not grow or make themselves. Trade routes supplied the materials needed to craft the tools of war.
questions.explanation: Trade brought new materials (copper, tin for bronze), ideas, and wealth. Trade routes also became sources of conflict — blocking them was a military strategy.
opinionPrompts: How did trade affect both cooperation and warfare?
What materials came through trade routes?

questions.question: Why was the land where ancient Mesopotamia was located called the 'Fertile Crescent'?
questions.evidence: Ancient Mesopotamian civilization started between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers. That land came to be known as the Fertile Crescent.
questions.explanation: The land was called 'Fertile Crescent' because it had rich soil for farming (fertile) and the area on a map was shaped like a crescent.
opinionPrompts: How did the rivers help people settle there?

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The Ubaid were the first settlers of Mesopotamia who transitioned from nomadic life to farming settlements and trade.
keyDetails: They were the first to settle the land known as Sumer
They drained marshes, grew crops, and built canals to irrigate drier areas
They made pottery and wove baskets from river materials
Trade routes from Asia and Europe brought goods they couldn't produce themselves
distractors: They lived in large cities with ziggurats

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Uruk was the most important city-state in Mesopotamia, where writing and organized society first developed.
keyDetails: Uruk had up to 80,000 people with large mud-brick buildings and a ziggurat
Pictographs appeared as symbols used in writing for trade records
Cuneiform replaced pictographs — symbols stood for sounds, not objects
King Gilgamesh became an epic hero and built the wall protecting Uruk

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: City-states fought for control until King Sargon united them into the Akkadian Empire.
keyDetails: City-states fought each other for control, making war common
Bronze weapons were made from copper and tin brought through trade routes
King Sargon of Akkad united the city-states into an empire in about 2340 bce
The empire stretched from the Mediterranean to Arabia at its peak
distractors: The empire lasted until modern times

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Peace under the Akkadian Empire led to major innovations that influenced the modern world.
keyDetails: The first mail system used clay tablets in sealed clay envelopes
The potter's wheel led to the invention of wheeled carts for transport
They developed numerical place value and base-60 system
We still use base-60 today: 60 seconds in a minute, 60 minutes in an hour
distractors: The mail system used paper letters

intro.title: Civilized
intro.content: Having rules and social order that help people live and work together. Not just living in families or tribes!

intro.title: Summarize
intro.content: A summary tells the MOST IMPORTANT details: Who? What? When? Where? Why? Leave out the small stuff!

ubaid.title: Nomadic
ubaid.content: Moving from place to place without a permanent home. The Ubaid STOPPED being nomadic when they settled down!

uruk.title: Ziggurat
uruk.content: A big stepped temple in Mesopotamia. Like a pyramid with steps on the outside and a temple on top!

uruk.title: Cuneiform
uruk.content: Wedge-shaped writing symbols. It replaced pictographs and could write ANY language because symbols stood for SOUNDS!

uruk.title: Adjectives
uruk.content: 'rich plains of Sumeria' — 'rich' is an adjective describing the noun 'plains'. Adjectives tell which one, how many, or what kind!

warring.title: Stele
warring.content: A tall stone slab with carved designs. The Stele of the Vultures shows the earliest record of Sumerian warfare!

warring.title: Empire
warring.content: Many nations or people ruled by ONE government. Sargon created the first empire by uniting all the city-states.

prosperity.title: Adjectives
prosperity.content: 'first mail system' — 'first' is an adjective telling which one. Find adjectives on pages 8-11!

prosperity.title: Abstract
prosperity.content: Relating to ideas, not physical things. Numbers and language are abstract concepts — you can't touch them!

fall.title: Raids
fall.content: Sudden surprise attacks. The Gutians raided the kingdom, making it unsafe to travel or farm.
